titleStmt.title: Letter from Matthew Nathan to Andrew Philip Magill, 22 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Matthew Nathan

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from the under-secretary Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939) to Andrew Philip Magill (1871-1941), the private secretary for the Chief Crown Secretary of Ireland, Augustine Birrell (1850-1933). In the letter Nathan is sending Magill the original report of a incident between the Irish Volunteers and the police on the 22 March 1916. The attack happened at an anti-Sinn Féin demonstration which took place in Tullamore, Co Offaly. Fourteen Irish Volunteers were besieged by a hostile mob in the local Sinn Féin hall. Believing they were inadequately protected by the police they fired revolvers to keep the mob away, and when the police moved in to disarm them they resisted arrest, and shots were fired seriously wounding Sergeant Ahearn. The report is from the head policeman at the scene.

msIdentifier.collection: NAI; Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers; CSO RP/1916/8125/5

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
CHIEF SECRETARY'S OFFICE.
DUBLIN CASTLE. 22/3/16 Dear Magill, I want the original report
on the Tullamore incident for
the moment so send the
attached copy for Mr. Birrell
to see. Yours sincerely Matthew Nathan
(Copy). Attempted murder by shooting &c.
R. v. Joseph Rafter; Joseph Morris;
Henry McNally & Thomas Byrne TULLAMORE,
King's Co. 21st March 1916. I beg to report that on last night an anti-Sinn Fein
demonstration was made through the principal streets in
Tullamore by about 200 women, men and boys. They halted
opposite the Sinn Fein rooms in William Street where the
Irish Volunteers also hold meetings and drill. The Police
moved the crowd away several times, but it reformed after
short intervals and made demonstrations of ill-feeling towards
the Irish Volunteers, calling them "Germans" and such names
as they thought would annoy them. They principally con-
sisted of the mothers, wives, brothers and sisters of men
who are fighting in the British Army at the front. The night was dark and the public lamps were not lit
in the town. It was therefore difficult for the Police
to see what the individual members of the crowd were doing.
Stones were thrown at the Sinn Fein rooms and revolver shots
were fired from the rooms in retaliation. At about 10.30 p.m. matters became threatening. I went
to William Street and with the available force I moved the
greater part of the crowd to a distance from the rooms and
gave instructions to the police to keep the people there and
induce them to go home. I then entered the Sinn Fein rooms
with Sergt. Philip Ahern to investigate the allegation of
shooting. (1) James O'Brennan, one of the leaders of the Irish
Volunteers, with 15 others whose names are as
follows, was present :-
(2) Joseph Grehan
(3) Joseph Rafter
(4) Peter Bracken
(5) Frank Brennan
(6) Joseph Morris
(7) John Delaney
(8) John Spain
(9) James Clarke
(10) Henry McNally
(11) Thomas Byrne
(12) Thomas Hogan
(13) Thomas Deigan
(14) Edward Downe
(15) John Martin; and
(16) John O'Flynn - all aged between 19 and 35 Years. O'Brennan complained of the conduct of the crowd
and showed me a few stones that he stated came through
the windows of their room. I informed him I required
the names of all present in the room, and by my direction
Sergeant Ahern noted them in his book. At this time
Co. Inspector Crane & Head-Const. Stuart arrived in the
room and the Co. Inspector gave orders to search the
party for arms in connection with the shooting. The
Sinn Feiners objected. Sergeant Ahern got hold of Frank
Brennan and the Co. Inspector went to his assistance.
There was a scuffle in the room, and immediately eight
or ten of the Sinn Feiners drew revolvers and fired
deliberately at us. We got mixed up with them, and the
firing continued by some while others attacked us with
hurleys and sticks. We were not armed. While I was
endeavouring to wrench a revolver from James O'Brennan
which he had aimed, and from which he had fired two shots
at the Co. Inspector and Sergeant Ahern, I was struck on
the head and arm and dazed with a hurley. My left arm
was powerless for the remainder of the fight, but
I wrenched the hurley from my assailant and kept him
prisoner with my right arm. The Co. Inspector was several-
ly
severally injured in the face and head, presumably from blows
of a heavy instrument and his clothes show marks of at least
one discharged bullet. Sergeant Ahern had to be conveyed
to hospital with his right arm broken and two bullets in his
arm and side. Head Const. Stuart got off with slight
injuries to his face & hands. It was a desperate attempt
on our lives, and it is marvellous how we escaped as we did,
for at least 20 shots were fired, and intended for us, but it
is rumoured some of them lodged in our assailants. We arrested
four of the Sinn Feiners in the room and afterwards conveyed
them under armed escort to the barracks where I charged them
with firing at and attacking us with intent to murder. When
cautioned they made no statements. Their names are given in front and they have been
remanded in custody until 11.15 a.m. on Monday, 27th instant
. Two revolvers & an automatic pistol were found in the room -
one revolver on prisoner Rafter. None of the other offenders have gone to their homes and
I am trying to locate them and arrest them. I consider as we may have to search other persons' houses
it is safer to procure warrants for their arrest. On examination to-day of the Sinn Feiners' room there were
found 15 bullet marks on the wall, varying from 3 to 5 feet
high from the floor. There is also a bullet mark through
the plate glass window across the street on Mr. Scally's
drapery establishment. Sergt. Ahern was conveyed by motor by direction of Doctor
Moorhead to Steven's Hospital at 3 p.m. to-day for X-Ray
treatment. I have communicated with the Commandant to have
him watched, and his dying declaration taken, if necessary.
I shall send copies of the evidence in due course, and I request
that the Crown Solicitor of this County be directed to take
charge of the prosecution. J. FITZGERALD
2 D. I. LK.
